items.fix: Use `(42,)` — the trailing comma is what creates a tuple.
items.why: Plain parentheses are just grouping; a comma is the tuple constructor.

items.mistake: Using a tuple of lists as a dict key.
items.fix: Convert any mutable inner element to a hashable equivalent (tuple, frozenset).
items.why: A tuple is hashable only if all its elements are hashable; one mutable element makes the whole thing unhashable.
items.example:
```python
key = ("chat", tuple(stop_tokens))   # OK
# key = ("chat", stop_tokens)        # TypeError: unhashable
```

items.mistake: Using a tuple just because you want immutability, but losing field names.
items.fix: Use a `namedtuple` or `@dataclass(frozen=True)` so each field has a meaningful name.
items.why: Positional tuples obscure intent; readers shouldn't have to remember 'index 2 is the temperature'.
items.example:
```python
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Cost:
    prompt: int
    completion: int
```

items.mistake: Treating a tuple as a fully-immutable, deep-frozen value.
items.fix: Remember that a tuple freezes its references, not the objects they point to. A `(list,)` whose list mutates still 'changes' visible state.
items.example:
```python
box = ([],)
box[0].append(1)   # legal — inner list still mutates
```
